children.text: And there were several- She had several run-ins with those nuns. I know she did. She had several run-ins with them. And I remember some of those run-ins. I was not a very good student. They remember me to this day, they remember me. What can I say?
How did your mom feel about you being punished for speaking Spanish?

She was upset. You know my grandfather, her father, my grandfather, didn'tt want us to learn English. He didn't- he just said, “You’re not coming into my house speaking English. You speak Spanish in here.” So, yeah. He was really, you know, strict on that. Did he know how to speak English? Yes, he knew how to speak English! He knew how to read, he knew how to sign his name, he knew all this stuff. You know, he just didn't want that- “I don’t want that language in my house.”
Why do you think that was?
I do not know, preservation? Self-preservation? You know, it was anger also. And it- he went to World War I and he was, I'm sure, experienced a lot of discrimination, so it just came down. You, know.
Let’s talk about your high school years. Did you also go to a parochial school?
Well, yes. The one year I went to public school, I kind of got kicked out. Believe it or not- for playing hooky. They caught me (laughter).

It was Kennedy, John Kennedy High School. It was a relatively new high school. Pretty modern. You know, I exceeded- let’s put it this way- the limit of absences. Me and one of the other activists- Domingo Bueno and his girlfriend at the time, his wife now, Maria Luisa, Boo Boo. We used to call her Boo Boo. He used to drive. So, we would go to Medina Lake and just hang out there and go fishing. We just- we weren't do anything like stealing or anything like that. We were just being teenagers- and wayward teenagers. And let me tell you, and this is pretty bad, we use to sneak out right under our teachers noses because our classroom was towards the back. Towards the parking lot. We had a teacher who was very cross-eyed, and we sat up front and she couldn't see us. We would just get up and walk out on her. I think one time she realized we weren'tt there (laughter). Anyway, we (laughter)– that sounds horrible I know. So, they caught us at Medina. And then the counselor went and talked to my dad and my mom and said that I could go to Edgewood High School. But, my father said no and he would send me back to Catholic school.
Why didn’t your father want to send you to Edgewood?
Because Edgewood was a bad school. That was uh, that was pretty bad.
What do you mean by bad school?

I mean not only were- it was poorly run, let’s put it that way. And it was a subpar education. So, they put me back at Little Flower. I graduated from Little Flower High School. The high school doesn't exist but the school still does. My picture is still there. That is Little Flower right there in the office on Zamora and Culebra. "What year did you graduate?" ’68, 1968. "Is that the same year you went into college?" Yeah, I went that fall.
Yeah, the reason I ask is because 1968, the year you graduated was a pretty big year in terms of student walkouts at Edgewood and student protests at Lenier.
At Lenier High School. Yeah, we were involved in that too. And I don't belive it was '68, I believe it was in ’69 when the walkouts took place, at Lenier.
And by that time, I had an internship at Guadalupe Community Center under Senator Joe Bernal. And Joe Bernal, I'm sure you know, was one of the co-sponsors of the state’s Bilingual Education Bill.

I was studying Bilingual Ed to become a Bilingual Ed teacher, at Our Lady of the Lake under this Project Excellence. Project Teacher Excellence. And Mr. Rosas who was in charge of that program was the one that came to Little Flower to speak to us because all of a sudden Our Lady of the Lake was going to be open to everyone. It was not going to be this exclusive convent school. Except when I went they still wanted to run it like a convent school. I mean here I am, I am coming from the barrio. The only way I could get to Our Lady of the Lake was to go downtown from my house and then come all the way back. You know, there was no bus service. I started walking. I mean hell. You know, I am not going to spend that money. It is not that far, to me. Walking was not that far. Just go down 26th Street, go straight, and you are right there. We use to see Our Lady of the Lake at night, from the trees, from the house. From the roof of the house where we climb trees. We use to think it was the castle. Mr. Rosas got us in there, into college and for a while I think I was pretty serious. I think my first semester I was serious.

House Bill 103 repealed Texas's English-only laws in 1969. Before then, it was illegal to teach in any language other than English in the state of Texas. It followed the federal Bilingual Education Act of 1968, which provided funding (grants to school districts) for bilingual education programs.

Decades of English-only policies and Americanization resulted in many Mexican-American children losing their Spanish. Parents (and grandparents) insisting that their children speak Spanish was resistance against this.
